title: Slow-Cooked Jam Sponge Pudding

overview.description: A soft steamed sponge pudding cooked slowly over raspberry or blackberry jam until light, tender and rich with fruit.

method: Grease a 1.5 litre pudding basin or heatproof bowl.
Spoon the jam into the bottom of the basin.
Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together until pale and fluffy.
Beat in the eggs one at a time.
Fold in the self-raising flour, baking powder and salt.
Add the milk and vanilla extract.
Mix until you have a soft dropping consistency.
Spoon the sponge mixture gently over the jam.
Cover the basin securely with a layer of baking parchment followed by foil.
Place the basin inside the slow cooker.
Pour boiling water into the slow cooker until it reaches approximately halfway up the sides of the basin.
Cover the slow cooker with its lid.
Cook on HIGH for approximately 3 hours, or until the sponge is risen and cooked through.
Carefully remove the basin from the slow cooker.
Turn the pudding out onto a serving plate, allowing the jam to run over the sponge.

learning.did-you-know: Steamed sponge puddings are descendants of older British boiled puddings, but using a covered basin allows the sponge to cook gently in a moist environment without drying out.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a soft steamed sponge and how slow, moist cooking produces a tender pudding.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A simple, comforting family pudding that can cook slowly while the main meal is being prepared and works particularly well with the family's weekly pudding tradition.
chapman-tips: The batter should be soft enough to drop easily from a spoon.
Don't overmix after adding the flour.
Cover the pudding basin securely so condensation does not drip onto the sponge.
The water should reach roughly halfway up the basin rather than covering it.
Check the water level during a long cook if necessary.
Use blackberry jam for a particularly fruity version.
The pudding is best served hot with plenty of custard.
A skewer inserted into the centre should come out clean when the sponge is cooked.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely, wrap securely and freeze in portions.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently in the microwave or by steaming. Excellent with custard.
